question_text: The diagram shows an electric dipole consisting of charges +q and −q separated by distance 2a. An arrow labelled p⃗ is drawn. In which direction does the dipole moment vector point?
image_alt: Two charges: −q on the left and +q on the right, separated by distance 2a. An arrow labelled p⃗ is shown between them.
explanation: By convention, the electric dipole moment vector p⃗ points from the negative charge to the positive charge. Its magnitude is p = q × 2a. This is a fundamental definition that must be memorised.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Perpendicular to the line joining the charges
Along the external electric field direction

elimination_hints.hint: This is the opposite of the convention. Current flows from + to − but dipole moment is defined from − to +.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the dipole moment direction convention

elimination_hints.hint: The dipole moment lies along the axis joining the two charges, not perpendicular to it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing dipole axis with equatorial direction

elimination_hints.hint: The dipole moment is a property of the dipole itself, not of the external field. It may or may not align with E⃗.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing dipole moment with field-induced alignment

question_text: The diagram shows an electric dipole placed in a uniform electric field E⃗ directed to the right. The dipole makes an angle θ with the field. At the position shown, the dipole will experience:
image_alt: A uniform electric field E⃗ pointing to the right. A dipole (−q on lower-left, +q on upper-right) makes angle θ with the horizontal field direction. Torque arrow shows clockwise rotation tendency.
explanation: In a uniform field, the net force on a dipole is zero (equal and opposite forces on +q and −q). However, the forces create a torque τ = pE sinθ that tends to align the dipole with the field. The torque is maximum when θ = 90° and zero when θ = 0° (aligned).
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: A net force along the field and no torque
A net force perpendicular to the field and a torque
Zero net force and a torque tending to align it with the field
Zero net force and zero torque

elimination_hints.hint: In a UNIFORM field, the forces on +q and −q are equal in magnitude but opposite. Net force is zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that uniform field means zero net force on dipole

elimination_hints.hint: There is no net force in a uniform field. Net force only arises in a non-uniform field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing uniform and non-uniform field effects

elimination_hints.hint: The torque is zero only when the dipole is aligned (θ = 0°) or anti-aligned (θ = 180°). At any other angle, τ = pE sinθ ≠ 0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming zero torque at all positions

question_text: The graph shows the variation of electric field E with distance r along the axial line of a short electric dipole. Which curve correctly represents this variation for r >> a?
image_alt: Four curves on an E vs r graph: (A) E ∝ 1/r (hyperbola), (B) E ∝ 1/r² (steeper curve), (C) E ∝ 1/r³ (steepest curve), (D) E = constant (horizontal line).
explanation: For a short dipole at large distances (r >> a), the axial field is E = 2kp/r³, which varies as 1/r³. This is a faster decrease than a point charge (1/r²). Curve C correctly shows this 1/r³ dependence. The equatorial field also varies as 1/r³ but is half the axial field.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: E ∝ 1/r is the dependence for an infinite line charge, not a dipole.
E ∝ 1/r² is the dependence for a point charge. A dipole field falls off faster because the fields of +q and −q partially cancel.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying point charge formula to dipole

elimination_hints.hint: A constant field only occurs between plates of a parallel plate capacitor, not for a dipole.

question_text: The diagram shows an electric dipole placed in a non-uniform electric field that increases in the +x direction. The dipole moment is along +x. The dipole will experience:
image_alt: Non-uniform electric field with arrows of increasing length pointing right. A dipole with −q on the left and +q on the right is aligned along +x. The field at +q is stronger than at −q.
explanation: In a non-uniform field, the force on +q (at the stronger field region) exceeds the force on −q (at the weaker field region). Since both forces are to the right, the net force is to the right (toward stronger field). For a dipole aligned with the field, it moves toward the region of stronger field. Additionally, since the dipole is already aligned (θ = 0), the torque is zero.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Zero net force and a torque
A net force toward the weaker field region
A net force and a torque both toward stronger field
A net force toward the stronger field region and zero torque

elimination_hints.hint: Zero net force occurs only in a uniform field. In a non-uniform field, the forces on +q and −q are unequal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying uniform field result to non-uniform field

elimination_hints.hint: When p⃗ is aligned with E⃗, the dipole is attracted toward the stronger field region, not repelled from it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction of net force

elimination_hints.hint: The torque is τ = pE sinθ. Since the dipole is already aligned (θ = 0), sinθ = 0 and the torque is zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming torque exists even when dipole is aligned

question_text: The SI unit of electric dipole moment is ______.
explanation: Electric dipole moment p = q × d, where q is charge (coulomb) and d is distance (metre). Therefore, SI unit = C·m (coulomb-metre). It is a vector quantity directed from −q to +q.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: C/m (coulomb per metre) is related to linear charge density, not dipole moment. Dipole moment = charge × distance, not charge ÷ distance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Dividing instead of multiplying charge by distance

elimination_hints.hint: N/C is the unit of electric field, not dipole moment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing dipole moment with electric field

elimination_hints.hint: V·m is equivalent to N·m²/C which relates to electric flux, not dipole moment.

question_text: Arrange the following orientations of an electric dipole in a uniform electric field in order of increasing potential energy (from most negative to most positive):

items.text: Dipole aligned parallel to E⃗ (θ = 0°): U = −pE

explanation: Potential energy U = −pE cosθ. At θ = 0°: U = −pE (minimum, most stable). At θ = 60°: U = −pE cos60° = −pE/2. At θ = 90°: U = −pE cos90° = 0. At θ = 180°: U = −pE cos180° = +pE (maximum, most unstable). The order from most negative to most positive is: −pE < −pE/2 < 0 < +pE.
subtopic: Potential energy of dipole in field
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This places θ = 180° before θ = 90°. Since U(180°) = +pE > U(90°) = 0, this order is wrong.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect ordering of potential energy values

elimination_hints.hint: This is the DECREASING order (most positive first). The question asks for INCREASING order (most negative first).
This places θ = 90° (U = 0) as the lowest energy, but θ = 0° (U = −pE) has the lowest (most negative) energy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming perpendicular orientation has minimum energy
